questions.prompt: Report the statement: She said, "I am reading a fascinating book." → She said she ___.

options.text: is reading a fascinating book
was reading a fascinating book
had reading a fascinating book
reads a fascinating book

questions.explanation: Present continuous 'am reading' backshifts to past continuous 'was reading' in reported speech. 'Is reading' keeps the original present tense without backshifting — a common error. 'Had reading' is not a grammatical form. 'Reads' is the backshift of present simple, not present continuous.

options.text: He told that the meeting was postponed.
She said me the news immediately.
They told us the flight had been cancelled.
He said to me told her the truth.

questions.explanation: 'Tell' requires a personal object: 'told us' is correct. Option A uses 'told that' without a personal object — 'told' must be followed by a person before 'that'. Option B uses 'said me' — 'say' cannot take a direct personal object; the correct form would be 'She told me the news' or 'She said the news to me'. Option D is grammatically incoherent.

questions.prompt: Report the question: "Have you ever been to Morocco?" → He asked me ___.

options.text: had I ever been to Morocco
if I had ever been to Morocco
if I have ever been to Morocco
whether had I ever been to Morocco

questions.explanation: Yes/no questions in reported speech use 'if' or 'whether', restore statement word order, and backshift: present perfect 'have been' shifts to past perfect 'had been'. Option A omits 'if/whether' and keeps inverted order. Option C keeps the present perfect without backshifting. Option D uses 'whether' but then keeps inverted order — the verb 'had' must come after 'I', not before.

questions.prompt: Report the command: "Please don't use your phones during the exam." → The invigilator told the students ___.

options.text: to not use their phones during the exam
don't use their phones during the exam
not to use their phones during the exam
that they didn't use their phones during the exam

questions.explanation: Negative reported commands use 'not to + infinitive', with 'not' placed before 'to': 'not to use'. Option A splits the infinitive by inserting 'not' between 'to' and 'use' — this is incorrect for reported commands. Option B keeps the bare imperative from direct speech. Option D changes the command into a past statement, losing the imperative force.

questions.prompt: Which reported sentence contains BOTH a correct backshift AND a correct time-word change?

options.text: She said she would finish the project the next day.
She said she will finish the project the next day.
She said she would finish the project tomorrow.
She said she finishes the project the next day.

questions.explanation: 'Will' backshifts to 'would', and 'tomorrow' shifts to 'the next day' — both changes are correctly applied in option A. Option B keeps 'will' unbackshifted. Option C correctly backshifts 'will' to 'would' but fails to shift 'tomorrow' to 'the next day'. Option D uses the present simple 'finishes' — neither the modal nor the time word is correctly transformed.

questions.prompt: Direct: "I was waiting for the bus when I saw the accident." → He told me he ___ for the bus when he ___ the accident.

options.text: was waiting / saw
had been waiting / had seen
was waiting / had seen
had been waiting / saw

questions.explanation: In backshift, both verbs move one tense back: past continuous 'was waiting' → past perfect continuous 'had been waiting', and past simple 'saw' → past perfect 'had seen'. So: he had been waiting for the bus when he had seen the accident. The other options each leave a verb un-backshifted: 'was waiting / saw' shifts neither; 'was waiting / had seen' keeps the first in the present-tense-area; 'had been waiting / saw' leaves 'saw' un-backshifted.
